# semmcci

Ivan Jacob Agaloos Pesigan 2026-06-11

## Installation

You can install the CRAN release of `semmcci` with:

``` r

install.packages("semmcci")
```

You can install the development version of `semmcci` from
[GitHub](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci) with:

``` r

if (!require("pak")) install.packages("pak")
pak::pkg_install("jeksterslab/semmcci")
```

## Description

In the Monte Carlo method, a sampling distribution of parameter
estimates is generated from the multivariate normal distribution using
the parameter estimates and the sampling variance-covariance matrix.
Confidence intervals for defined parameters are generated by obtaining
percentiles corresponding to 100(1 - α)% from the generated sampling
distribution, where α is the significance level.

Monte Carlo confidence intervals for free and defined parameters in
models fitted in the structural equation modeling package `lavaan` can
be generated using the `semmcci` package. The package has three main
functions, namely,
[`MC()`](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/reference/MC.md),
[`MCMI()`](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/reference/MCMI.md),
and
[`MCStd()`](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/reference/MCStd.md).
The output of `lavaan` is passed as the first argument to the
[`MC()`](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/reference/MC.md)
function or the
[`MCMI()`](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/reference/MCMI.md)
function to generate Monte Carlo confidence intervals. Monte Carlo
confidence intervals for the standardized estimates can also be
generated by passing the output of the
[`MC()`](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/reference/MC.md)
function or the
[`MCMI()`](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/reference/MCMI.md)
function to the
[`MCStd()`](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/reference/MCStd.md)
function. A description of the package and code examples are presented
in Pesigan and Cheung (2023:
<https://doi.org/10.3758/s13428-023-02114-4>).

## Example

A common application of the Monte Carlo method is to generate confidence
intervals for the indirect effect. In the simple mediation model,
variable `X` has an effect on variable `Y`, through a mediating variable
`M`. This mediating or indirect effect is a product of path coefficients
from the fitted model.

``` r

library(semmcci)
library(lavaan)
```

### Data

``` r

summary(df)
#>        X                  M                  Y           
#>  Min.   :-2.98669   Min.   :-2.96235   Min.   :-2.81769  
#>  1st Qu.:-0.69798   1st Qu.:-0.68626   1st Qu.:-0.72267  
#>  Median :-0.09421   Median :-0.05612   Median :-0.04656  
#>  Mean   :-0.05336   Mean   :-0.03347   Mean   :-0.03456  
#>  3rd Qu.: 0.57321   3rd Qu.: 0.65431   3rd Qu.: 0.61166  
#>  Max.   : 2.96687   Max.   : 2.80003   Max.   : 3.05031  
#>  NAs    :100        NAs    :100        NAs    :100
```

### Model Specification

The indirect effect is defined by the product of the slopes of paths `X`
to `M` labeled as `a` and `M` to `Y` labeled as `b`. In this example, we
are interested in the confidence intervals of `indirect` defined as the
product of `a` and `b` using the `:=` operator in the `lavaan` model
syntax.

``` r

model <- "
  Y ~ cp * X + b * M
  M ~ a * X
  X ~~ X
  indirect := a * b
  direct := cp
  total := cp + (a * b)
"
```

### Monte Carlo Confidence Intervals

We can now fit the model using the
[`sem()`](https://rdrr.io/pkg/lavaan/man/sem.html) function from
`lavaan`. We use full-information maximum likelihood to deal with
missing values.

``` r

fit <- sem(data = df, model = model, missing = "fiml")
```

The `fit` `lavaan` object can then be passed to the
[`MC()`](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/reference/MC.md)
function to generate Monte Carlo confidence intervals.

``` r

mc <- MC(fit, R = 20000L, alpha = 0.05)
mc
#> Monte Carlo Confidence Intervals
#>              est     se     R    2.5%  97.5%
#> cp        0.2785 0.0308 20000  0.2182 0.3385
#> b         0.5058 0.0297 20000  0.4472 0.5638
#> a         0.4973 0.0311 20000  0.4368 0.5582
#> X~~X      0.9084 0.0426 20000  0.8245 0.9918
#> Y~~Y      0.5408 0.0268 20000  0.4887 0.5936
#> M~~M      0.7731 0.0374 20000  0.7004 0.8467
#> Y~1       0.0092 0.0252 20000 -0.0399 0.0590
#> M~1      -0.0071 0.0292 20000 -0.0645 0.0499
#> X~1      -0.0592 0.0313 20000 -0.1213 0.0020
#> indirect  0.2516 0.0213 20000  0.2111 0.2942
#> direct    0.2785 0.0308 20000  0.2182 0.3385
#> total     0.5300 0.0305 20000  0.4701 0.5901
```

### Monte Carlo Confidence Intervals - Multiple Imputation

The [`MCMI()`](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/reference/MCMI.md)
function can be used to handle missing values using multiple imputation.
The [`MCMI()`](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/reference/MCMI.md)
accepts the output of
[`mice::mice()`](https://amices.org/mice/reference/mice.html),
[`Amelia::amelia()`](https://rdrr.io/pkg/Amelia/man/amelia.html), or a
list of multiply imputed data sets. In this example, we impute
multivariate missing data under the normal model.

``` r

mi <- mice::mice(
  df,
  method = "norm",
  m = 100,
  print = FALSE,
  seed = 42
)
```

We fit the model using lavaan using the default listwise deletion.

``` r

fit <- sem(data = df, model = model)
```

The `fit` `lavaan` object and `mi` object can then be passed to the
[`MCMI()`](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/reference/MCMI.md)
function to generate Monte Carlo confidence intervals.

``` r

mcmi <- MCMI(fit, mi = mi, R = 20000L, alpha = 0.05, seed = 42)
mcmi
#> Monte Carlo Confidence Intervals (Multiple Imputation Estimates)
#>             est     se     R   2.5%  97.5%
#> cp       0.2786 0.0307 20000 0.2183 0.3386
#> b        0.5051 0.0297 20000 0.4474 0.5641
#> a        0.4971 0.0312 20000 0.4355 0.5582
#> X~~X     0.9108 0.0428 20000 0.8267 0.9944
#> Y~~Y     0.5408 0.0272 20000 0.4879 0.5942
#> M~~M     0.7724 0.0378 20000 0.6984 0.8463
#> indirect 0.2511 0.0213 20000 0.2106 0.2944
#> direct   0.2786 0.0307 20000 0.2183 0.3386
#> total    0.5297 0.0303 20000 0.4695 0.5892
```

### Standardized Monte Carlo Confidence Intervals

Standardized Monte Carlo Confidence intervals can be generated by
passing the result of the
[`MC()`](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/reference/MC.md)
function or the
[`MCMI()`](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/reference/MCMI.md)
function to
[`MCStd()`](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/reference/MCStd.md).

``` r

MCStd(mc, alpha = 0.05)
#> Standardized Monte Carlo Confidence Intervals
#>              est     se     R   2.5%  97.5%
#> cp        0.2662 0.0289 20000 0.2089 0.3223
#> b         0.5068 0.0271 20000 0.4521 0.5588
#> a         0.4745 0.0263 20000 0.4230 0.5255
#> X~~X      1.0000 0.0000 20000 1.0000 1.0000
#> Y~~Y      0.5442 0.0254 20000 0.4943 0.5940
#> M~~M      0.7748 0.0249 20000 0.7238 0.8211
#> indirect  0.0093 0.0183 20000 0.2051 0.2767
#> direct   -0.0072 0.0289 20000 0.2089 0.3223
#> total    -0.0621 0.0252 20000 0.4556 0.5543
```

``` r

MCStd(mcmi, alpha = 0.05)
#> Standardized Monte Carlo Confidence Intervals
#>             est     se     R   2.5%  97.5%
#> cp       0.2970 0.0288 20000 0.2095 0.3223
#> b        0.4832 0.0274 20000 0.4519 0.5590
#> a        0.4835 0.0266 20000 0.4206 0.5254
#> X~~X     1.0000 0.0000 20000 1.0000 1.0000
#> Y~~Y     0.5395 0.0257 20000 0.4937 0.5947
#> M~~M     0.7662 0.0252 20000 0.7239 0.8231
#> indirect 0.2337 0.0186 20000 0.2041 0.2772
#> direct   0.2970 0.0288 20000 0.2095 0.3223
#> total    0.5306 0.0250 20000 0.4561 0.5545
```

## Documentation

See [GitHub Pages](https://jeksterslab.github.io/semmcci/index.html) for
package documentation.

## Citation

To cite `semmcci` in publications, please cite Pesigan & Cheung (2024).

## References

Pesigan, I. J. A., & Cheung, S. F. (2024). Monte Carlo confidence
intervals for the indirect effect with missing data. *Behavior Research
Methods*, *56*(3), 1678–1696.
<https://doi.org/10.3758/s13428-023-02114-4>

R Core Team. (2026). *R: A language and environment for statistical
computing*. R Foundation for Statistical Computing.
<https://www.R-project.org/>

# Package index

## Functions

- [`Func()`](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/reference/Func.md) :
  Monte Carlo Confidence Intervals (List)
- [`MC()`](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/reference/MC.md) :
  Monte Carlo Confidence Intervals
- [`MCFunc()`](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/reference/MCFunc.md)
  : Monte Carlo Confidence Intervals (Function)
- [`MCGeneric()`](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/reference/MCGeneric.md)
  : Monte Carlo Confidence Intervals (Generic)
- [`MCMI()`](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/reference/MCMI.md) :
  Monte Carlo Confidence Intervals (Multiple Imputation)
- [`MCStd()`](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/reference/MCStd.md)
  : Standardized Monte Carlo Confidence Intervals

## Methods

- [`coef(`*`<semmcci>`*`)`](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/reference/coef.semmcci.md)
  : Parameter Estimates

- [`confint(`*`<semmcci>`*`)`](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/reference/confint.semmcci.md)
  : Monte Carlo Confidence Intervals for the Parameter Estimates

- [`print(`*`<semmcci>`*`)`](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/reference/print.semmcci.md)
  :

  Print Method for Object of Class `semmcci`

- [`summary(`*`<semmcci>`*`)`](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/reference/summary.semmcci.md)
  :

  Summary Method for an Object of Class `semmcci`

- [`vcov(`*`<semmcci>`*`)`](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/reference/vcov.semmcci.md)
  : Sampling Covariance Matrix of the Parameter Estimates

  Model](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/articles/example-3-latent.md):

  (FIML)](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/articles/example-4-1-simple-miss-fiml.md):

  Reliability](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/articles/example-5-composite-reliability.md):

  Coefficients](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/articles/mcstd-1-std-regression.md):

  Coefficients](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/articles/mcstd-2-difference-regression-coefficients.md):
- [MCStd Function Use Case 3: R-Squared and Adjusted
  R-Squared](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/articles/mcstd-3-rsqr.md):

  Coefficients in Multiple
  Groups](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/articles/mcstd-4-difference-regression-coefficients-multigroup.md):
- [MCStd Function Use Case 5: Difference of R-Squared in Multiple
  Groups](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/articles/mcstd-5-difference-rsq-multigroup.md):

  (MI)](https://github.com/jeksterslab/semmcci/articles/methods-mc-mi.md):
